explanation_md: `Inspector` is declared a friend of `Safe`, so all of `Inspector`'s member functions can access `Safe`'s private members. `reveal` reads `s.m_code` which was initialized to 7. The output is `7`.

prompt_md: Class `A` declares `friend class B;` inside its body. Which statement is true?

options.a: `A` can now access private members of `B`, and `B` can access private members of `A`.
options.b: `B` can access private members of `A`, but `A` cannot access private members of `B`.
options.c: All classes that inherit from `B` can also access `A`'s private members.
options.d: Any member function in `A` can now access private members of `B` through a `B` object.

explanation_md: Friendship is one-directional and non-inherited. Declaring `friend class B` inside `A` grants `B` access to `A`'s private members â€” not the other way around. `A` does not automatically get access to `B`'s private members. Classes derived from `B` do not inherit the friendship â€” they would have to be separately declared friends of `A`.

prompt_md: You want to grant only one specific function `void Printer::print(const Doc&)` access to `Doc`'s private members, rather than granting all of `Printer` access. What should you write inside `Doc`'s class body?
explanation_md: To grant access to a single member function of another class, use the fully qualified name of that member function in the `friend` declaration: `friend void Printer::print(const Doc&);`. Option (a) would grant all of `Printer`'s functions access, which is broader than desired. Option (d) would declare a standalone (non-member) free function `print` as a friend, not `Printer`'s member function.

options.b: `Chest` never declared `Explorer` (or `Explorer::loot`) as a friend, so `loot` cannot access `m_treasure`.
options.c: `m_treasure` must be in a `public:` section for any function to access it.
options.d: You cannot pass a class object to a free function by reference.

explanation_md: `m_treasure` is private in `Chest`. For `Explorer::loot` to access it, `Chest` must declare either `friend class Explorer;` or `friend void Explorer::loot(const Chest&);` inside its class body. Without that declaration, `loot` is an outsider and the access is a compile error. Option (c) is technically true but overly broad â€” making it `public` would fix the compile error but breaks encapsulation; the proper fix is `friend`.
